result:
The rain doesn't fall here the way it does in other places. In this city it arrives sideways, almost hesitant, like it's embarrassed to interrupt. I watch it from the window of a late-night ramen shop that never closes, where the owner—a man named Kenji who claims he was once a yakuza accountant—serves bowls with the quiet precision of someone performing a ritual.

There's a particular loneliness that only exists at 3:17 a.m. It's not the dramatic kind. It's softer, almost friendly. The kind that sits across from you at the counter and shares your noodles without asking. I've been coming here for eleven months now, ever since my marriage dissolved like sugar in hot broth. My wife kept the dog. I kept the regret.

Kenji doesn't speak much, but when he does, his sentences arrive fully formed and slightly devastating. Last week he told me, "The problem with running away is that you always arrive at yourself." Then he slid another beer across the counter without being asked.

I think about leaving sometimes. Buying a ticket to somewhere with better light. But the truth is I've grown fond of this particular shade of melancholy. The way the streetlights fracture in puddles. The sound of trains that no longer carry anyone I know. The gentle understanding that some stories don't need grand endings—they just need to be witnessed by someone who understands the beauty of ordinary things.

Maybe that's enough. Maybe the rain knows something we don't. Maybe tomorrow I'll order the spicy miso instead of the usual. Small rebellions matter too.

The kind that sits across from you at the counter and shares your noodles without asking. I've been coming here for eleven months now, ever since my marriage dissolved like sugar in hot broth. My wife kept the dog. I kept the regret.

Kenji doesn't speak much, but when he does, his sentences arrive fully formed and slightly devastating. Last week he told me, "The problem with running away is that you always arrive at yourself." Then he slid another beer across the counter without being asked.

I think about leaving sometimes. Buying a ticket to somewhere with better light. But the truth is I've grown fond of this particular shade of melancholy. The way the streetlights fracture in puddles. The sound of trains that no longer carry anyone I know. The gentle understanding that some stories don't need grand endings—they just need to be witnessed by someone who understands the beauty of ordinary things.

Maybe that's enough. Maybe the rain knows something we don't. Maybe tomorrow I'll order the spicy miso instead of the usual. Small rebellions matter too.
